items.prompt_md: In the dict + doubly-linked-list LRU design, what does the dict map each key to?
options: A copy of the value only
The doubly linked list node for that key, giving O(1) access to its position
The key's index in an array
The recency rank of the key as an integer
items.answer: The doubly linked list node for that key, giving O(1) access to its position
items.explanation_md: The dict's job is purely addressing: hand back the exact node object in O(1) so it can be unlinked and relinked without any scan.

items.prompt_md: You need get/put in O(1) on a fixed-capacity cache, evicting the least-recently-used entry on overflow. Which design fits?
options: A dict alone, scanning all entries to find the least-recently-used one on eviction
A dict mapping key to a node in a doubly linked list, so lookup is O(1) and recency order updates by unlinking/relinking in O(1)
A sorted list of (last-used-time, key) pairs, re-sorted after every access
A single array, shifting elements to keep the most-recent one at the front
items.answer: A dict mapping key to a node in a doubly linked list, so lookup is O(1) and recency order updates by unlinking/relinking in O(1)
items.explanation_md: Fixed capacity + recency-based eviction + O(1) get/put is the fingerprint for composing a hash map with a doubly linked list — neither structure alone gives O(1) both ways.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of get and put in the dict + doubly-linked-list LRU cache design?
options: O(n) for get, O(1) for put
O(1) for get, O(n) for put
items.explanation_md: Each call does one dict lookup plus O(1) pointer relinking at the list's sentinel ends — no scanning, ever — at the cost of O(capacity) extra space for the map and the list nodes.
